title: boston tea party version 2
description: Boston, December 16, 1773. The Sons of Liberty stand hours away from their boldest act of defiance — the destruction of 342 chests of East India Company tea aboard the Dartmouth. But a spy has whispered the plan to the customs men, and if the traitor is not unmasked before the ships are boarded, the ringleaders will hang before dawn. You must move through the fog-choked streets of Boston, question allies and suspects alike, and expose the informer before the operation — and the cause of liberty — is crushed forever.

pressureEvents: A customs officer is spotted near Griffin's Wharf — the threat scale rises by 2 and all NPC suspicion thresholds tighten.
A rider arrives at the Green Dragon Tavern with word that Governor Hutchinson has refused to let the ships leave — panic spreads and one random NPC becomes evasive until reassured.
Church bells begin to toll from the Old South Meeting House — the final rally has begun, and the boarding party will move in 5 minutes regardless of the investigation's outcome.

winConditions: Correctly accuse the spy before time expires, presenting at least one key evidence clue to support the accusation.
Present both key evidence clues and confront the spy directly, triggering a full confession that protects all ringleaders.
failConditions: Time expires without a final accusation — the customs men are tipped off and the boarding party is arrested on the dock.
The player makes a false accusation against an innocent character — the real spy escapes and alerts the authorities in time to stop the operation.
partialSuccessExamples: The player correctly names the spy but possesses only one key evidence clue — the spy is expelled from the Sons of Liberty but escapes prosecution, and the tea party proceeds under a cloud of uncertainty.

categories.name: Sons of Liberty Codes
categories.context: Used by Patriots and Sons of Liberty members when speaking in guarded company or passing messages through trusted hands at meeting houses and taverns.

terms.meaning: The disguised raiders are assembled and prepared to board the tea ships
A trusted Patriot sympathizer whose loyalty has been vouched for
The mass public meeting of colonists convened to decide the fate of the tea, held at Old South Meeting House
A merchant appointed to receive and sell the taxed tea — used contemptuously as a synonym for Crown collaborator
A rallying point and symbol; to be summoned 'to the tree' means an urgent assembly of Sons of Liberty is called
A suspected informer or loose-tongued member whose words have reached the wrong ears
The legal deadline by which the Dartmouth must clear customs or the Crown may seize both ship and cargo — the ticking clock forcing the Patriots' hand

categories.name: Customs House Cant
categories.context: Employed by royal customs officers, tide waiters, and Crown agents such as Agent Silas Dawes when conducting official business or communicating covertly with informants.

terms.meaning: A customs officer stationed aboard a ship at anchor to prevent unlawful unloading before official inspection
The formal declaration of a ship's cargo at the customs house — without it, no goods may legally land
Official paperwork completed allowing a vessel to depart; the Patriots were desperate to prevent this and force a standoff
A secret agreement by which a Crown informant is promised a share of seized goods or monetary reward in exchange for intelligence
A veiled threat implying arrest, imprisonment, or worse at the Crown's discretion
A legal instrument allowing customs officers to confiscate contraband or an uncleared vessel — feared by merchants and smugglers alike
A broad search authority despised by colonists as it allowed officers to search any premises without naming a specific target

categories.name: Dockworkers' and Mariners' Argot
categories.context: Spoken by sailors, longshoremen, and wharf hands at Griffin's Wharf and along the waterfront, including those employed aboard the Dartmouth under Captain Rotch.

terms.term: the Dartmouth's riding at single anchor
terms.meaning: The tea ship is moored alone and lightly secured — vulnerable to boarding
The 342 casks of East India Company tea stored in the ship's hold, the object of the night's action
To secure lines or hatches; used as slang among dockhands for keeping a secret tight

terms.term: the fog's thick enough to hide a regiment
terms.meaning: A common waterfront expression indicating conditions ideal for clandestine movement along the wharves

terms.term: a King's man on the quarterdeck
terms.meaning: A Crown loyalist or customs officer stationed aboard a vessel, watching for unauthorized activity
Dockworker shorthand for dumping cargo overboard quickly — repurposed that night for the tea destruction itself

categories.name: Tavern and Street Slang
categories.context: Common vernacular of Boston tradesmen, apprentices, and tavern-goers heard nightly at the Green Dragon Tavern under Margaret Cole's roof, where rumor and sedition flow as freely as ale.

terms.meaning: A sardonic toast or coded phrase among Patriots referencing the night's planned destruction; said with a knowing look among conspirators
A loyalist sympathizer or Crown informant suspected of roosting among Patriot company
A popular hot tavern drink of beer, rum, and molasses — the currency of conversation and bribery at the Green Dragon
Street-level rumor circulating through Boston's market district, often carrying a kernel of dangerous truth
A discreet after-dark mission whose true purpose is not spoken aloud in public company
Period expression for boasting or speaking too openly about a secret plan — a fatal indiscretion

terms.term: a long face at the wharf
terms.meaning: Local slang for someone behaving suspiciously nervous near the docks, possibly guilty of betrayal or forewarning the authorities

categories.name: Congregationalist and Pulpit Rhetoric
categories.context: The elevated moral and scriptural language used by Reverend Ezra Willard and other clergy when addressing their congregations or justifying — or condemning — Patriot actions in private counsel.

terms.meaning: New England's self-conception as a community bound to God and to one another by sacred obligation — invoked to justify resistance to unjust authority
Biblical reference to a traitor hidden within a righteous community whose concealed guilt brings ruin on all — used to describe the suspected informer
A person whose moral scruples prevent them from fully committing to a dangerous cause; Willard's own self-description
The belief that God would intervene to protect the Patriot cause if it was righteous — a comfort and a justification for bold action
A scriptural epithet for a treacherous or worthless person; the harshest clerical condemnation applicable to an informer
A politically charged religious address delivered on days of fasting and prayer, often serving as thinly veiled Patriot propaganda from the pulpit
